coredata.dc:title: Cellulose-derived hydrothermally carbonized materials and their emerging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Current Opinion in Green and Sustainable Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Adolfsson, Karin H.
Yadav, Nisha
Hakkarainen, Minna
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and its further modifications are intriguing materials for a wide range of potential applications. Hydrothermal carbonization is a sustainable process for converting biopolymers or other biomass sources into carbonaceous materials under mild conditions in water and at relatively low temperatures. This review presents the latest progress in modification and utilization of hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and related materials in environmental, biomedical, and energy applications. Further applications presented include evaluation of cellulose-derived carbon spheres or carbon dots as catalysts, antibacterial materials, flame retardants, and functional fillers in bioplastic composites. The wide range of applications highlights the great potential and multifunctionality of hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and its derivatives. The field is expected to further expand and increase in importance as we move toward circular bioeconomy.

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) of cellulose for production of carbon spheres (CSs). CSs were subsequently oxidized and reduced to fabricate nanographene oxide (nGO) and reduced nanographene oxide (r-nGO) type carbon dots (CDs), respectively.
Different applications demonstrated for biomass-derived hydrothermally carbonized materials.
Table over physicochemical properties of derived HTC materials and their derivatives and the corresponding application field.

row.entry:
HTC: 200 °C, 2 h, H2SO4 cat.
Post treating: 90 °C, 60 min, HNO3

HTC: 180 °C, 2 h, H2SO4 cat.
Post treating: 90 °C, 30 min, HNO3
HTC: 180 °C, 2 h, cat. caffeic acid

HTC: 220 °C, 2 h, cat. H2SO4
Post treating: 90 °C, 30 min, HNO3
HTC: 180 °C, 2 h, cat. caffeic acid

HTC: 220 °C, 2 h, cat. H2SO4
Posttreating: 90 °C, 30 min, HNO3

HTC: 180 °C, 2 h, cat. H2SO4
Post-treating: 90 °C, 30 min, HNO3

HTC: 220 °C, 2 h, cat. H2SO4, stirring
Post-treating: 90 °C, 0.5 h, HNO3, dialysis

head.ce:title: Cellulose-derived hydrothermally carbonized materials and their emerging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Fibre and Polymer Technology, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Teknikringen 56, 100 44 Stockholm, Sweden

head.ce:miscellaneous: This review comes from a themed issue on Green processes and technologies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and its further modifications are intriguing materials for a wide range of potential applications. Hydrothermal carbonization is a sustainable process for converting biopolymers or other biomass sources into carbonaceous materials under mild conditions in water and at relatively low temperatures. This review presents the latest progress in modification and utilization of hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and related materials in environmental, biomedical, and energy applications. Further applications presented include evaluation of cellulose-derived carbon spheres or carbon dots as catalysts, antibacterial materials, flame retardants, and functional fillers in bioplastic composites. The wide range of applications highlights the great potential and multifunctionality of hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and its derivatives. The field is expected to further expand and increase in importance as we move toward circular bioeconomy.

ce:sections.ce:para: For complete overview of the section, please refer the article collection - Green processes and technologies

ce:section.ce:para: There is a growing interest toward utilization of biomass as a resource for material applications. Among others, cellulose-derived carbonized materials have attracted huge interest and fascination [1,2]. There are essentially two main carbonization processes: slow pyrolysis and hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) [3]. The slow pyrolysis process is conducted by heating (300–650 °C) in the absence of oxygen at slow heating rates (10–30 °C min−1) and long residence times (5 min–12 h). HTC, a thermochemical conversion process, is performed in neutral or acidic aqueous solutions at relatively low temperatures (180–260 °C) typically only under autogenic pressure [2]. In both of the processes, liquid and gas products are also formed. HTC is similar to slow pyrolysis performed at slow heating rates and long residence times [3]. Great advantages of HTC over slow pyrolysis are that wet biomass may be used as feedstock, the formation of harmful gases during processing is limited, and the auto-ignition of solids is low due to many surface oxygen groups [3] .

HTC has emerged as an especially attractive route to multifunctional carbonized materials [4], due to the sustainable processing conditions. Both carbonization processes lead to carbonized materials with higher energy content and lower O/C and H/C ratios than those of the feedstock [3]. The cellulose-derived HTC materials are typically amorphous spherical particles, for example, carbon spheres (CSs) with sizes ranging from micrometer to nanometer [5]. The formed CSs may partly be interconnected, and the surface area and porosity of CSs are generally significantly smaller than those of materials carbonized by slow pyrolysis process [1]. In addition to CSs, flake-like structures have also been encountered after HTC [6]. The HTC materials have content of both aromatic and aliphatic groups and typically contain oxygen functionalities such as COOH, OH, C=O, and C–O [5]. The feedstock influences the chemical composition. As an example, HTC of Salix biomass produced carbonized material with N functional groups [7]. HTC materials have typically higher O/C and H/C ratios and lower ash content than those of carbonized materials from the slow pyrolysis process. All this influence the suitability in different applications (Table 1).

HTC also offers the possibility to produce carbon dots (CDs), which have emerged as a new carbon nanostructure [8–11]. CDs are typically around 10 nm in diameter [12], but they can also be somewhat larger [13]. An interesting property of CDs is the exhibition of photoluminescence (PL). To produce CDs from cellulose, an oxidation process of CSs was developed as a subsequent step after carbonization [14,15]. This led to cutting of CSs into nanographene oxide (nGO)–type CDs (Figure 1 ). It was shown that the oxidation process increased the O content compared with CSs and led to CDs with excellent dispersion stability in water. For applications demanding lower degree of oxygenation, a green reduction reaction was developed [17]. The reduction was performed with help of superheated water in the microwave with or without caffeic acid as a green reducing agent. In other cases, the CD has been produced through the in situ approach typically using small molecules such as glucose [12,16]. The CDs have also been produced directly through HTC of carbohydrates [17] and more complex biomass compositions [13,18].

The properties of hydrothermally carbonized biomass and cellulose, such as renewable origin, tuneable surface characteristics, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, and low cost, in combination with the facile and green production route pave the way for multiple application possibilities. In this review, the recent progress in application of cellulose-derived hydrothermally carbonized materials in different fields is covered, including environmental, biomedical, and energy applications among others (Figure 2 ). Furthermore, the new promising application as functional filler in bioplastic composites is also highlighted.

Pharmaceutical residues and heavy metals are emerging pollutants in aquatic environments. The presence of these pollutants in the ecosystem can lead to unpredictable ecological impacts and may also have a negative impact on human health. The removal of the pollutants by conventional wastewater treatment methods has proven to be ineffective. Lately, different carbon products from active carbon to graphene oxide have been demonstrated as powerful adsorbents for environmental contaminants. The low-cost adsorbents produced by HTC of cellulose and other biomass sources could, thus, offer a route to economical and effective remediation of wastewater from both organic and inorganic contaminants.

In general, the specific surface area, pore structure, and surface functionalities of carbon materials determine the adsorption efficiency. As an example, carbonized material derived from Salix biomass with plenty of oxygen and nitrogen surface functional groups induced effective adsorption of Cr (VI) from aqueous solution [7]. The maximum adsorption efficiency was reached at low pH as the functionalities became protonated and could electrostatically attract the Cr (VI) anions. The several oxygenated surface functional groups serve to improve the adsorption capacity of HTC materials. Furthermore, they are useful for chemical activation [19]. One work demonstrated increased adsorption efficiency for carbonized material from pinewood sawdust after being treated with H2O2 [20]. The activated carbonized material showed 42 times increased uptake of Pb2+ ions. The cellulose-derived CSs were capable of adsorbing several organic contaminants including common inflammatory drugs (diclofenac sodium (DCF) and ketoprofen) and fungicides (diphenylamine) [21]. Furthermore, KOH-activated carbon, hydrothermally produced from municipal woody and herbaceous prunings, was demonstrated as a powerful adsorbent for the removal of DCF from aqueous samples [22].

nGO-type CDs, produced by oxidation of cellulose-or chitosan-derived CSs, also enhanced the adsorption capacity of chitosan hydrogels [23]. The robust 3D cross-linked networks of the hydrogel composites demonstrated complete or near complete DCF removal from water. In addition, the added CD could catalyze the chitosan hydrogel cross-linking reaction by genipin. The adsorption of methylene blue was studied on HTC material derived from bamboo in acidic solution, followed by NaOH postactivation [24]. The adsorption capacity of the activated carbonized material was 655.8 mg g−1 at a temperature of 30 °C. The improvement in adsorption capacity after NaOH activation was suggested to be due to the introduction of additional oxygen functionalities. In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) produced from rice husks were used for monitoring ions through PL quenching. GQD was shown to be highly selective toward Fe3+ among several studied ions [18].

To mitigate the CO2 quantities in the atmosphere, the carbonized materials could serve as adsorbents for capturing CO2. The hydrothermally treated material is economical but can require certain posttreatment strategies for effective adsorption of CO2. Woody and herbaceous prunings were subjected to HTC followed by activation in KOH at higher temperatures [22]. The as-produced carbonized material was tested as CO2 adsorbent and showed uptake of 84.5 mg CO2 g−1. The hydrothermally carbonized bioresources can, thus, open up the scope for environmentally friendly and cost-efficient materials for treating and monitoring wastewater, as well as CO2 capturing.

The utilization of carbonized materials in biomedical applications has been promoted by their generally good biocompatibility, in combination with other interesting properties and multifunctionality. Bone tissue engineering has emerged as a great tool for restoring and repairing bone defects after an injury. Conventional single-component polymers are generally not able to meet the requirements of biomedical applications requiring bioactivity or other functionalities. The incorporation of cellulose-derived reduced nGO (r-nGO) in polycaprolactone nanocomposites could induce bioactivity in the form of calcium phosphate mineralization on the surface of the composite films [25]. At the same time, the good biocompatibility of the films as determined by cell viability testing against osteoblast-like MG63 cells was retained. r-nGO also provided favorable reinforcement of the composite films. Other studies showed similar positive effects for carbonized starch-derived nGO-modified starch scaffolds [26]. nGO grafted on the surface of porous polycaprolactone scaffolds also induced biomineralization [27], at the same time as it provided adsorption sites enabling drug loading and delivery. CDs are also highly interesting materials for different imaging applications because of the PL property. One study demonstrated the production of carbon quantum dot–grafted silica nanoparticles (silica–C NPs) with intense PL through HTC processing of rice husks, which has high content of silica [28]. The as-produced silica–C NPs were water dispersible, surface modifiable, biocompatible, and stable, altogether showing prominent features for bioimaging applications.

Energy shortage, environmental crisis, and customer demands have driven people to find low-cost, environment friendly, and nontoxic routes to develop functional materials. For instance, the use of carbon materials in capacitors is widely renowned. Thus, HTC materials from biomass are highly attractive for energy applications. Furthermore, the low ash content in HTC materials [29] means less risk of fouling, slagging, and corrosion during combustion [3]. The carbonized materials can either be used as a medium for storing energy in capacitors or used as a solid fuel, which is directly burned for energy [29]. An inkjet printing process was developed for the production of ‘all-graphene’ microsupercapacitors based on graphene and an ultrathin solid-state electrolyte of nGO CDs derived from CSs [30]. The devices had areal capacitances of up to 313 μF cm−2 and high-power densities of up to ~4 mW cm−3 with a thickness of only ~1 μm. Cellulose-derived CSs from corn straw were also used as an anode in lithium-ion batteries [31]. The cycling stability was excellent, and the device showed a specific capacity of 577 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.2°C.

Several works have demonstrated that hydrothermally carbonized materials have promising outlooks as solid fuels [32–37]. The temperature greatly impacted the energy recovery efficiency of HTC-processed algal biomasses and sludge [38]. A higher temperature increased the energy density and decreased the mass yield giving rise to an efficiency optimum at 210 C. The energy content of carbonized material derived from olive oil mill wastewater was further evaluated [39]. It was shown that the carbonized material displayed higher energy content with decreasing water-to-sludge ratio, whereas time did not have large influence on the final properties.

Apart from the aforementioned applications, carbonized materials are emerging as highly interesting materials for wide range of fields. As an example, the utilization of carbonized materials for fabrication of composite materials and as antibacterial components is of great interest. Other carbon materials such as graphene-based fillers are already well known for improving mechanical and other properties, such as flame retardancy of polymer composites.

The main purpose of adding cellulose-derived carbonized materials is to enhance one or multiple properties of the material. nGO CDs were applied as coadditives with polyamidoamines (PAA) to advance the flame retardancy of cotton fabrics [40]. The addition of nGO CDs resulted in synergetic effect with PAA through radical scavenging. The combination could inhibit and extinguish the flame in vertical flame spread tests to a greater extent than the sole use of PAA or nGO CDs. Cellulose-derived nGO-type CDs were also effective nucleating agents for polylactide (PLA) composites [15]. Already at low loadings of 0.05 wt% in PLA composites, the addition of CDs improved the mechanical and O2 barrier performance compared with neat PLA and PLA composited with conventional graphene oxide. Furthermore, a thermoplastic ‘all-cellulose’ composite was enabled by covalent attachment of oxidized carbonized materials to cellulose acetate (CA) [6]. The transparent thermoplastic ‘all-cellulose’ composite showed good film-forming ability and had improved mechanical and optical performance than that of neat CA.

Carbon materials have also attracted increasing attention as antimicrobial materials. Cellulose-derived HTC CSs were treated with acidic and basic solutions to evaluate the influence of different functionalities on the antibacterial properties of CSs [41]. The acid-treated CSs showed antibacterial activity toward both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which was suggested to be due to the surface functionalities and their interactions with the bacteria. The base-treated CSs did not show any antibacterial activity.

The promise of carbonized materials for catalysis and agroenvironmental applications has earlier been on the agenda and is still of high interest. In the field of catalysis, there is a need of creating effective catalysts for synthesis of materials and degradation of chemical contaminants from an environmental perspective. The carbonized supported aluminum material was, for example, applied as a catalyst for glucose isomerization reaction, and the glucose to fructose conversion was shown to have 78 mol% selectivity [42]. In agroenvironmental applications, it is of importance to improve the surface characteristics and to reduce any environmental pollution from the carbonized materials. It has been shown that the feedstock and carbonization process (HTC or pyrolysis) affect the content and properties of the obtained carbonized material [43]. Another way is to ameliorate the hydrothermally carbonized materials by posttreatments. For example, carbonized material from poplar sawdust was microbially aged to increase the amount of free amino acids and to reduce the amount of toxic compounds, before addition to rice paddy soil [44]. Furthermore, HTC of olive oil mill waste produced a liquid phase with sufficient amount of micronutrients, which was promising for facilitating plant growth [32].

The hydrothermal carbonization process is a sustainable route to biomass-derived carbonaceous materials. It is performed in water, which removes the necessity of predrying, and it operates at low temperatures. This review covers the recent progress in the area of cellulose-derived hydrothermally carbonized materials and their emerging applications. While hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose typically leads to nanometer- to micrometer-sized CSs, different postmodifications such as oxidation and reduction can be applied for fabrication of nanometer-sized CDs with varying content of oxygen functionalities. The surface chemistry promotes a variety of possible postmodifications. Furthermore, the feedstock content and processing conditions also offer great possibilities for tuning the properties of the HTC materials. The large-scale production, however, is still a challenge that needs further investigations. The hydrothermally carbonized cellulose and its derivatives are multifunctional materials, and we are just starting to reveal their huge potential in wide range of applications. These hydrothermally carbonized materials have already been applied in a variety of different fields, including the environmental, biomedical, and energy sectors. Moreover, new applications are rapidly emerging, including the use as property enhancers and inducers in composites and as antibacterial materials. In bioplastic and biopolymer composite applications, CDs have been demonstrated as multifunctional additives which able to, among others, improve the adsorption capacity of hydrogels toward environmental contaminants, induce bioactivity, and improve the mechanical and barrier properties. Furthermore, thermoplastic ‘all-cellulose’ materials were enabled by covalent attachment of oxidized carbonized cellulose to CA main chain. These examples indicate great potential for HTC materials in tackling some of the great challenges, such as inadequate properties, preventing the wider use of bioplastic, and biopolymers as replacements for traditional plastic materials. The great potential of hydrothermally carbonized cellulose, its derivatives, and other HTC materials in multiple applications is already clear and we have probably still only scratched on the surface. The interest and new application fields will continue to grow with promising future outlooks as we move toward bioeconomy and zero waste.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.H. and N.Y. are grateful for the financial support from the Wallenberg Wood Science Center financed by Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation, Sweden.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomass-derived carbonaceous materials: recent progress in synthetic approaches, advantages, and applications
ACS Sustain Chem Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-P. Yang, Z. Li, M. Liu, X. Zhang, Y. Chen, H. Xue, E. Ye, R. Luque, Biomass-derived carbonaceous materials: recent progress in synthetic approaches, advantages, and applications, ACS Sustain Chem Eng 7 (2019) 4564-4585. doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.8b06030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass waste for hydrochar formation: process conditions, fundamentals, and physicochemical properties
Renew Sustain Energy Rev

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Wang, Y. Zhai, Y. Zhu, C. Li, G. Zeng, A review of the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass waste for hydrochar formation: process conditions, fundamentals, and physicochemical properties, Renew Sustain Energy Rev 90 (2018) 223-247. doi:10.1016/j.rser.2018.03.071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparative review of biochar and hydrochar in terms of production, physico-chemical properties and applications
Renew Sustain Energy Rev

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.S. Kambo, A. Dutta, A comparative review of biochar and hydrochar in terms of production, physico-chemical properties and applications, Renew Sustain Energy Rev 45 (2015) 359-378. doi:10.1016/j.rser.2015.01.050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphological and structural differences between glucose, cellulose and lignocellulosic biomass derived hydrothermal carbons
Green Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Falco, N. Baccile, M.M. Titirici, Morphological and structural differences between glucose, cellulose and lignocellulosic biomass derived hydrothermal carbons, Green Chem. 13 (2011) 3273-3281. doi:10.1039/c1gc15742f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The production of carbon materials by hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sevilla, A.B. Fuertes, The production of carbon materials by hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose, Carbon. 47 (2009) 2281-2289. doi:10.1016/j.carbon.2009.04.026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermoplastic “all-cellulose” composites with covalently attached carbonized cellulose
Biomacromolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.H. Gustavsson, K.H. Adolfsson, M. Hakkarainen, Thermoplastic “all-cellulose” composites with covalently attached carbonized cellulose, Biomacromolecules. (2020). doi:10.1021/acs.biomac.9b01672.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: This interesting article addresses the redesign of biopolymers into bioplastics with the help of covalently attached cellulose-derived hydrothermally carbonized particles. The authors examine the film-forming ability, optical and mechanical performance of the produced thermoplastic “all-cellulose” derived composites. The work also paves a possible way for upcycling paper waste products taking steps towards a circular bioeconomy.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel nitrogen enriched hydrochar adsorbents derived from Salix biomass for Cr (VI) adsorption
Sci Rep

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lei, H. Su, F. Tian, A novel nitrogen enriched hydrochar adsorbents derived from Salix biomass for Cr (VI) adsorption, Sci Rep 8 (2018) 1-9. doi:10.1038/s41598-018-21238-8.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: This comprehensive work opens up for removal of trace amounts of metals from aqeuous solutions using hydrothermally cabronized materials derived from complex biomass. The environmental and economical value could be benefited by moving over to hydrothermally carbonized materials as efficient adsorbents.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: the next generation platform for biomedical applications
Mater Sci Eng C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ghosal, A. Ghosh, Carbon dots: the next generation platform for biomedical applications, Mater Sci Eng C 96 (2019) 887-903. doi:10.1016/j.msec.2018.11.060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on the photocatalysis of carbon dots: classification, mechanism and applications
Nano Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Han, S. Zhu, S. Lu, Y. Song, T. Feng, S. Tao, J. Liu, B. Yang, Recent progress on the photocatalysis of carbon dots: classification, mechanism and applications, Nano Today. 19 (2018) 201-218. doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2018.02.008.

J Nanobiotechnol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sharma, J. Das, Small molecules derived carbon dots: synthesis and applications in sensing, catalysis, imaging, and biomedicine, J Nanobiotechnol 17 (2019) 1-24. doi:10.1186/s12951-019-0525-8.

Green Chem

Sci Rep

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Papaioannou, A. Marinovic, N. Yoshizawa, A.E. Goode, M. Fay, A. Khlobystov, M.M. Titirici, A. Sapelkin, Structure and solvents effects on the optical properties of sugar-derived carbon nanodots, Sci Rep 8 (2018) 1-10. doi:10.1038/s41598-018-25012-8.

J Struct Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.N. Egorova, A.E. Tomskaya, A.N. Kapitonov, A.A. Alekseev, S.A. Smagulova, Hydrothermal synthesis of luminescent carbon dots from glucose and Birch Bark Soot, J Struct Chem 59 (2018) 780-785. doi:10.1134/S0022476618040054.

ACS Sustain Chem Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.B. Erdal, K.H. Adolfsson, T. Pettersson, M. Hakkarainen, Green strategy to reduced nanographene oxide through microwave assisted transformation of cellulose, ACS Sustain Chem Eng 6 (2018) 1245-1255. doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.7b03566.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zero-dimensional and highly oxygenated graphene oxide for multifunctional poly(lactic acid) bionanocomposites
ACS Sustain Chem Eng

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots from sugars and ascorbic acid: role of the precursors on morphology, properties, toxicity, and drug uptake
ACS Med Chem Lett

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Cailotto, E. Amadio, M. Facchin, M. Selva, E. Pontoglio, F. Rizzolio, P. Riello, G. Toffoli, A. Benedetti, A. Perosa, Carbon dots from sugars and ascorbic acid: role of the precursors on morphology, properties, toxicity, and drug uptake, ACS Med Chem Lett 9 (2018) 832-837. doi:10.1021/acsmedchemlett.8b00240.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of cellulose-based carbon dots for bioimaging
ChemistrySelect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Shen, J. Gao, J. Cong, Z. Liu, C. Li, J. Yao, Synthesis of cellulose-based carbon dots for bioimaging, ChemistrySelect. 1 (2016) 1314-1317. doi:10.1002/slct.201600216.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots from rice husks for Fe3+ sensing
Ind Eng Chem Res

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Wang, Z. Wang, J. Liu, Y. Peng, X. Yu, W. Wang, Z. Zhang, L. Sun, One-pot facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots from rice husks for Fe3+ sensing, Ind Eng Chem Res 57 (2018) 9144-9150. doi:10.1021/acs.iecr.8b00913.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: In this paper the valorisation of rice husks is conducted using hydrothermal carbonization. The obtained carbon quantum dots were applied for Fe3+ sensing, which shed light on environmental applications, such as water purification, for hydrothermally carbonized materials and derivatives.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal conversion of biomass waste to activated carbon with high porosity: a review
Chem Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Jain, R. Balasubramanian, M.P. Srinivasan, Hydrothermal conversion of biomass waste to activated carbon with high porosity: a review, Chem Eng 283 (2016) 789-805. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2015.08.014.

Bioresour Technol

Chem Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Puccini, E. Stefanelli, A.L. Tasca, S. Vitolo, Pollutant removal from gaseous and aqueous phases using hydrochar-based activated carbon, Chem Eng Trans 67 (2018) 637-642. doi:10.3303/CET1867107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biobased nanographene oxide creates stronger chitosan hydrogels with improved adsorption capacity for trace pharmaceuticals
ACS Sustain Chem Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Feng, A. Simeone, K. Odelius, M. Hakkarainen, Biobased nanographene oxide creates stronger chitosan hydrogels with improved adsorption capacity for trace pharmaceuticals, ACS Sustain Chem Eng 5 (2017) 11525-11535. doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.7b02809.

Ecotoxicol Environ

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.C. Qian, X.P. Luo, X. Wang, M. Guo, B. Li, Removal of methylene blue from aqueous solution by modified bamboo hydrochar, Ecotoxicol Environ 157 (2018) 300-306. doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2018.03.088.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of bioactive and reinforced bioresorbable nanocomposites by reduced nano-graphene oxide carbon dots
Biomacromolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.B. Erdal, M. Hakkarainen, Construction of bioactive and reinforced bioresorbable nanocomposites by reduced nano-graphene oxide carbon dots, Biomacromolecules. 19 (2018) 1074-1081. doi:10.1021/acs.biomac.8b00207.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: This extensive work displays the construction of cytocompatible carbon dot-PCL nanocomposites with propery enhancements, including bioacitivty and mechanical reinforcement. Showing the potemtial of using hydrothermally carbonized materials and derivatives in biomedical applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Starch-derived Nnanographene oxide paves the way for electrospinnable and bioactive starch scaffolds for bone tissue engineering
Biomacromolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wu, A. Samanta, R.K. Srivastava, M. Hakkarainen, Starch-derived Nnanographene oxide paves the way for electrospinnable and bioactive starch scaffolds for bone tissue engineering, Biomacromolecules. 18 (2017) 1582-1591. doi:10.1021/acs.biomac.7b00195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cellulose-derived nanographene oxide surface-functionalized three-dimensional scaffolds with drug delivery capability
Biomacromolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.B. Erdal, J.G. Yao, M. Hakkarainen, Cellulose-derived nanographene oxide surface-functionalized three-dimensional scaffolds with drug delivery capability, Biomacromolecules. 20 (2019) 738-749. doi:10.1021/acs.biomac.8b01421.

J Mater Chem B

Sun, Photoluminescent carbon quantum dot grafted silica nanoparticles directly synthesized from rice husk biomass, J Mater Chem B 5 (2017) 4679-4689. doi:10.1039/c7tb00811b.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Minireview of potential applications of hydrochar derived from hydrothermal carbonization of biomass
J Ind Eng Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Fang, L. Zhan, Y.S. Ok, B. Gao, Minireview of potential applications of hydrochar derived from hydrothermal carbonization of biomass, J Ind Eng Chem 57 (2018) 15-21. doi:10.1016/j.jiec.2017.08.026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fully inkjet printed ultrathin microsupercapacitors based on graphene electrodes and a nano-graphene oxide electrolyte
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sollami Delekta, K.H. Adolfsson, N. Benyahia Erdal, M. Hakkarainen, M. Ostling, J. Li, Fully inkjet printed ultrathin microsupercapacitors based on graphene electrodes and a nano-graphene oxide electrolyte, Nanoscale. 11 (2019) 10172-10177. doi:10.1039/c9nr01427f.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: In this paper the utilization of carbon dots from cellulose as electrolytes was examined in "all-graphene" inkjet printed microsupercapacitors. Demonstrating the use of new carbon materials to maximize the performance and minimize the cost.

Nanomaterials
